1: A type: 'the LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.' Jesus explicitly claims this title: 'I am the good shepherd' (John 10:11,14). The type is direct: YHWH the shepherd of Ps 23 is the role Jesus claims as his own, making the identification between YHWH and Jesus explicit in John 10. The 'I shall not want' — the complete sufficiency of the shepherd's provision — is realized in the abundance Christ gives: 'I came that they may have life and have it abundantly' (John 10:10).
2: A type: 'he makes me lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside still waters.' The shepherd's provision — rest and water. Christ's provision of rest: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). The 'living water' of John 4:10-14: 'whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again.' The still waters of Ps 23 become the rivers of living water that Jesus promises (John 7:38).
3: A type: 'he restores my soul. He leads me in paths of righteousness for his name's sake.' 'Restores my soul' — the healing of the whole person, not just external circumstances. Christ's restorative work: the prodigal son's return ('he came to himself,' Luke 15:17), the healing of the paralyzed man (soul and body: 'your sins are forgiven... rise,' Mark 2:5-11). 'Paths of righteousness for his name's sake' — the shepherd leads in covenant integrity for the sake of his own reputation; Christ is himself the path: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life' (John 14:6).
4: A type: 'even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me.' Christ is both the one who walks through death's valley and the shepherd who accompanies others through it. The incarnation and crucifixion are YHWH's own shepherd-walk through the valley of death — 'though he die, yet shall he live' (John 11:25). Hebrews 2:14-15: Jesus shared flesh and blood so 'that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death... and deliver all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery.'
5: A type: 'you prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows.' The prepared table anticipates the Eucharist: the covenant meal set by the host for the guest, the anointing of the honored guest, the overflowing cup. The Last Supper is set explicitly in the presence of enemies — Judas is at the table, and the cross is hours away. The 'cup that overflows' becomes the cup of blessing (1 Corinthians 10:16). Christ is both the host who prepares the table and the lamb who is the feast.
6: A type: 'surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life, and I shall dwell in the house of the LORD forever.' 'Goodness and mercy' (tov vachesed) — the two covenant qualities that 'follow' (pursue, escort) the sheep. In Christ, these qualities are embodied: 'full of grace and truth' (John 1:14). 'The house of the LORD forever' (le'orekh yamim) — the eternal dwelling anticipated by John 14:2-3: 'In my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you... that where I am you may be also.' Ps 23 ends where the gospel ends: eternal habitation with YHWH.
